stem: A hospital uses an HbA1c test to screen for Type 2 diabetes. The test has a sensitivity of 93% and a specificity of 92%. The prevalence of Type 2 diabetes in the patient population is estimated to be between 0.8% and 1.5%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has Type 2 diabetes? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(Type 2 diabetes | positive test) ranges from 0.0857 to 0.15 as prevalence varies from 0.8% to 1.5%. Point estimate at 1.1% prevalence: 0.114.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.08.
The confidence interval [0.0857, 0.15] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (93%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
